nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing hill streams and rivers with rocky substrates, clear well-oxygenated water, and moderate to strong current; also found in slower lowland rivers and reservoirs during certain seasons. Predominantly associated with the Western Ghats mountain range.

decorPreferences: smooth river pebbles and rounded substrate
driftwood with trailing plants (Java fern, Anubias)
moderate water movement / powerhead or spray bar
dense planting along tank sides and back

diet.notes: Accepts virtually all aquarium foods: high-quality flake, micro-pellets, frozen or live daphnia, brine shrimp, bloodworms, and mosquito larvae. Small invertebrates and insect larvae form much of the wild diet alongside algae and plant matter. Feed 2–3 times daily; varied diet supports best coloration and condition.

avoidWith: small nano fish that may be outcompeted at feeding
Giant Danio (Devario aequipinnatus) — frequent misidentification; both species may compete heavily in smaller tanks

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo for Devario malabaricus; verify attribution and license before use.

summary: The Malabar Danio (Devario malabaricus) is one of the larger danios, reaching up to 10 cm, and is native to the hill streams of India’s Western Ghats and Sri Lanka. It tends to be peaceful in community aquariums when kept in shoals of six or more, prefers cool, well-oxygenated water with good flow, and is commonly confused with the Giant Danio (Devario aequipinnatus).
careNotes:
The Malabar Danio is one of the larger members of the Danionidae family, reaching up to 10 cm in length. It originates from the fast-flowing hill streams and rivers of India’s Western Ghats — a biodiversity hotspot — and also occurs in Sri Lanka. Coming from these environments, it does best in cooler temperatures (18–26°C) and well-oxygenated, moderately soft water; temperatures above 26°C sustained long-term should be avoided. A powerhead, spray bar, or canister filter with a strong return is recommended to replicate the moderate current of its native habitat.

A minimum tank of 150 L with a 120 cm footprint is advisable to accommodate its active swimming behaviour and allow a proper shoal. Keep in groups of at least 6; smaller groups tend to result in skittish behaviour and can increase inter-fish aggression. The Malabar Danio is a known jumper — a tight-fitting lid is essential.

The species is frequently misidentified as or sold alongside the Giant Danio (Devario aequipinnatus), which is superficially similar but slightly larger, with different stripe patterning. Devario malabaricus has a characteristic reticulated blue-and-gold lateral pattern with a more fragmented, irregular stripe compared to the bolder, more continuous stripes of D. aequipinnatus. Males are slimmer and more intensely coloured; females are deeper-bodied and larger.

The species is generally considered adaptable and undemanding within its preferred parameters, accepting all standard aquarium foods, and is well-suited to a community biotope featuring other South Asian species such as hillstream loaches, barbs, and rainbowfish.
breedingNotes: The Malabar Danio is an egg scatterer that tends to spawn readily in captivity when well-conditioned. To encourage spawning, separate a group of well-fed adults at a 2:1 female-to-male ratio into a shallow breeding tank furnished with fine-leaved plants or a spawning mop positioned over a mesh bottom. Spawning typically occurs in the morning in response to a temperature drop — a 20% cool water change is the most common trigger — or after a conditioning period on live or frozen foods. Eggs are adhesive and will be eaten by adults if left in place; parents should be removed promptly after spawning, or a mesh separator used to protect the eggs below. Eggs hatch in 24–48 hours at 24°C, and fry are free-swimming within 3–4 days, at which point they accept infusoria and baby brine shrimp nauplii. Breeding is generally considered straightforward, though a dedicated setup improves fry yield significantly.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Devario malabaricus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 10 cm), temperature range (18–26°C), pH (6.5–7.5), hardness, native range (Western Ghats, India; Sri Lanka), habitat (fast-flowing hill streams, clear oxygenated water), diet (omnivore: invertebrates, plant matter), shoaling behaviour, and tank requirements. Also noted frequent confusion with Devario aequipinnatus.

additional.name: FishBase — Devario malabaricus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Danionidae, Cypriniformes), adult size (TL to 10 cm), native range (India, Sri Lanka, Western Ghats), habitat and depth. Confirmed cool-water preferences and omnivorous diet. Synonym list includes Cyprinus malabaricus Hamilton, 1822.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Danio Care Guide
additional.notes: General danio care guidance; confirmed easy care level, shoaling minimum group size of 6, omnivorous diet (flake, pellets, frozen foods), active mid-water swimming behaviour, and high escape risk requiring covered tanks for danio species.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Devario species profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source. Confirmed 18–26°C temperature preference distinguishing D. malabaricus from higher-temperature danios, active shoaling behaviour, Western Ghats origin, and common confusion with Giant Danio (D. aequipinnatus). Notes on breeding as egg scatterer, spawning triggers, and fry care.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Devario malabaricus (Malabar Danio)
additional.notes: Trade and husbandry notes; confirmed fin-nipping tendency toward slow long-finned tankmates, high escape risk, and distinguishing morphological features from D. aequipinnatus. Noted importance of strong water movement and cool temperatures for long-term health.

fish.breedingNotes: An egg scatterer that tends to spawn readily when conditioned on live or frozen foods and triggered with a cool water change. Fine-leaved plants or a spawning mop over a mesh bottom are recommended; adults should be removed after spawning to protect the eggs. Eggs hatch in 24–48 hours, and fry accept baby brine shrimp nauplii from around day 3–4.
